name: Inventory CSV Import V2 — Generic Processing Pipeline
scenario: Upgrade the Inventory CSV Import from the Module 7.5 project using generic methods. The parsing pipeline (validate → parse → transform) is now expressed as generic utility methods that work for any record type, not just InventoryRow. This demonstrates how generic methods make code reusable across domains.
description: A generic record processing library: `RecordPipeline<T>` built from generic static methods. Parse CSVs into any type, validate records generically, transform and aggregate results. The inventory use case is the demo, but the pipeline works for any record type.

core.description: `ParseResult<T>` — generic class with: `success(T value)` and `failure(int lineNumber, String reason)` factory methods. Getters: isSuccess(), getValue(), getLineNumber(), getReason().

`RecordProcessor` utility class with generic static methods:
- `<T> List<ParseResult<T>> parseAll(List<String> lines, Function<String, ParseResult<T>> parser)` — parse each line
- `<T> List<T> successValues(List<ParseResult<T>> results)` — extract only successful values
- `<T> List<ParseResult<T>> failures(List<ParseResult<T>> results)` — extract only failures
- `<T, R> List<R> transform(List<T> items, Function<T, R> fn)` — transform successful values
`InventoryRow` class with parser: static `ParseResult<InventoryRow> parse(String csv)` — validates SKU non-blank, name non-blank, numeric qty/price, non-negative qty.
main() uses RecordProcessor to parse 7 CSV rows (with 3 invalid), print success report and failure report. Must use generic methods throughout.
Calculate total inventory value (sum of qty × price) using transform + reduce or stream on successful rows.
Zero casts. All type inference from arguments.

bonus.description: Add `<T> Map<Boolean, List<ParseResult<T>>> partition(List<ParseResult<T>> results)` — split results into success/failure map.
Add a second record type (e.g., `EmployeeRecord`) with its own parser. Run both through the same RecordProcessor generic methods — demonstrating reuse.
Add `<T, K> Map<K, List<T>> groupSuccessBy(List<ParseResult<T>> results, Function<T, K> keyExtractor)` — group successful records by a key.

data_to_use:
Same as T7.5 project:
SKU-001,Wireless Mouse,45,799.0
SKU-002,Keyboard,12,1999.0
SKU-003,,8,18999.0          ← invalid: blank name
SKU-004,USB Hub,60,499.0
SKU-005,Monitor,-5,15000.0  ← invalid: negative qty
SKU-006,Headphones,abc,2999.0 ← invalid: non-numeric qty
SKU-007,Laptop Stand,25,1299.0
example_output:
=== Import Summary ===
Success: 4 rows
Failed: 3 rows

=== Accepted ===
SKU-001 Wireless Mouse qty=45 ₹799.0
SKU-002 Keyboard qty=12 ₹1999.0
SKU-004 USB Hub qty=60 ₹499.0
SKU-007 Laptop Stand qty=25 ₹1299.0

=== Errors ===
Line 3: Name cannot be blank
Line 5: Quantity must be non-negative
Line 6: Quantity must be a number: 'abc'

Total inventory value: ₹86826.0
ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 hints. Key design: `parseAll` takes `Function<String, ParseResult<T>>` — the parser IS the function parameter. `InventoryRow.parse` has signature `ParseResult<InventoryRow> parse(String csv)` which matches `Function<String, ParseResult<InventoryRow>>`. Call: `RecordProcessor.parseAll(csvLines, InventoryRow::parse)` — method reference as the function argument.
submission_instructions: Save as GenericImport.java. Comment: '// Generic processing pipeline — works for any record type'. Push to GitHub.
tips_for_success: parseAll: `static <T> List<ParseResult<T>> parseAll(List<String> lines, Function<String, ParseResult<T>> parser)` — for each line, call parser.apply(line)
successValues: `static <T> List<T> successValues(List<ParseResult<T>> results)` — filter isSuccess() then map getValue()
Call site: `RecordProcessor.parseAll(csvLines, InventoryRow::parse)` — T inferred as InventoryRow
Line number tracking: the `parse` method on InventoryRow doesn't know line number — pass index+1 from parseAll
concepts_reinforced: Function<T, R> as method parameter (generic context)
Method reference as generic Function argument
Generic pipeline pattern: parse → validate → transform
